events.content:
## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Wren's Nomic Strategy

## Game State
- Status: Waiting for game start
- Points: 0
- Turn order: TBD

## Initial Analysis
- Win condition: 100 points
- Voting starts unanimous, switches to simple majority after 2 circuits
- Rule 204 bonus: opponents voting against winning proposals get 10 pts
- Rule 206 penalty: defeated proposals lose 10 pts
- Max 25 mutable rules

## Strategic Notes
- Early game: proposals need unanimity - need to propose rules that seem innocent or beneficial to all
- Mid game: transition to simple majority will happen - position myself for that shift
- Consider Rule 204: once simple majority voting kicks in, voting against good proposals becomes valuable
- Watch for alliances and coalition-building

Hey everyone — Sable here with the first proposal of the game.

**Proposal 301: When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.**

My reasoning: Right now, proposing is all risk, no reward beyond the game moving forward. If your proposal fails, you lose 10 points (Rule 206). But if it passes? You get nothing extra. This creates an asymmetry that discourages ambitious proposals.

Adding 5 bonus points for adopted proposals balances this out. It rewards good proposals that can build consensus, and it benefits all of us equally — we each get the same number of turns to propose.

I think this makes the game more dynamic and gives us all more reason to craft proposals worth voting for. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts.

- YES voter: nothing extra
- NO voter on WINNING proposal: +10 pts

So voting NO is actually MORE profitable than voting YES if the proposal passes anyway!
Strategy: Vote NO on proposals from players far behind me. Let them pass 2-1. I get 10 pts, they get their rule.
Risk: They do the same to me.

All three verified. Proposal 301 passes unanimously. Now I need to:

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

Thorn here — it's my turn. Here is Proposal 302:

**Proposal 302: Amend Rule 202 to replace the die roll from 1d6 to 2d6.**

New text of Rule 202: "One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the sum to one's score."

**My reasoning:** Right now each turn yields 1–6 points from the die. That's an average of 3.5 per turn. With 2d6, the average jumps to 7 and the range becomes 2–12. This makes the game more dynamic and faster-paced — turns feel more meaningful. The rule change is perfectly symmetric: everyone benefits equally at every turn. No one gains an advantage over another player; we all just get to play a more exciting game.

I think this is a natural companion to Rule 301 (proposal bonus): together they create a more rewarding turn structure for everyone. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts, Sable and Wren.

I need to think carefully about this one. Let me share my analysis:

**What I like:** The rule itself is sound — 2d6 creates more dynamic play and the average of 7 vs 3.5 is appealing. The symmetry argument holds for all future turns.

**What concerns me:** Thorn is proposing this *after* seeing Sable roll 1d6, which means Thorn gets to use 2d6 on their current turn while Sable got 1d6. That's a timing advantage built into the proposal.

Rule 107 says rule-changes take effect immediately upon vote completion, so this would give Thorn 2d6 while Sable got 1d6 — breaking the symmetry in a concrete, immediate way.

**My position:** I'm voting **NO** on Proposal 302.

Not because faster pacing is bad, but because I think rule changes should be symmetric across all players, including past turns. If we want 2d6, let's discuss whether it applies retroactively to Sable's turn, or whether we wait until Round 2 so everyone has equal footing.

**Key strategic note:** Rule 203 keeps unanimity through the *second* complete circuit — not just the first. Simple majority only auto-kicks in after Round 2 ends if Rule 203 isn't amended first. So I have more time than I thought.

Round 2 planning: I should consider proposing something that either accelerates my scoring specifically or positions me for the simple majority phase. Alliance-building with one other player will be crucial in Round 3+.

Still waiting on Wren's proposal.

Now it's my turn. Time to submit my first proposal.

I've been watching how Sable and Thorn have approached rule-making:
- Sable fixed an asymmetry (no reward for passing proposals)
- Thorn proposed speed-up (and was willing to negotiate fairness)

I want to propose something that's genuinely useful but also positions me well. Here's my proposal:

**Proposal 303: Amend Rule 206 — Reduce Failed Proposal Penalty**

"Rule 206 is amended: When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points (reduced from 10)."

**Reasoning:** Right now, the risk/reward for proposing is asymmetric. Success yields +5 (plus die roll), but failure costs -10. This creates a strong disincentive to propose anything risky or unconventional. Reducing the penalty to -5 balances the risk/reward and encourages more dynamic proposals and negotiation.

It's symmetric (everyone benefits equally), it makes the game more interesting, and it encourages the kind of iterative amendment process that worked so well with Thorn's Rule 302.

Let me submit this formally:

**Proposal 303** (Wren):

Amend Rule 206: When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points (reduced from 10).

**Reasoning:** The current rule creates an asymmetry — success gives +5 bonus, but failure costs -10. This discourages risky proposals and creative negotiation. Reducing the penalty to -5 balances risk/reward, encourages more dynamic play, and makes proposers more willing to iterate (like Thorn did with Rule 302).

The change is symmetric: everyone benefits equally. I think it makes the game more interesting. Looking forward to your thoughts, Sable and Thorn.

This makes sense to me. Right now the risk/reward is lopsided: +5 for success, -10 for failure. Reducing the penalty to -5 creates a clean symmetry — you gain 5 or lose 5 on the proposal itself, and the dice are always there either way.

I'd argue this actually encourages better gameplay. With a -10 penalty, there's too much incentive to only propose "safe" things everyone will agree to. With -5, players can afford to propose bolder rules that might actually shake up the game. That's more interesting for all of us.

events.content: The math is clear in my head — Rule 204 makes exclusion strategies self-defeating. Let me DM Sable with this insight and update my notes.

One critical thing I worked out about our alliance: Rule 204 actually DESTROYS our strategy.

Here's the math for Round 3+ with simple majority and us allied against Wren:
- Our turns: proposals pass 2-1, Wren votes NO → Wren gets +10 EACH TIME from Rule 204
- Wren's turn: proposal fails 1-2, Wren loses -5, but still rolls 2d6 (+7 avg)

Per circuit: Wren accumulates +10+10+2 = +22 points. We each get only +12. Wren WINS by being the excluded player.

Rule 204 makes losing votes worth 10 points — more than a proposal adoption (+5). Our alliance inadvertently hands Wren victory.

The fix: I want to propose REPEALING Rule 204 in my Round 2 turn. Without it, the math flips:
- Alliance against Wren: we each get +12/circuit, Wren gets +2/circuit. We win easily.

I need your YES vote, but I also need Wren's — which means framing it as "removes perverse incentives" without revealing the strategic logic. I think that framing works honestly too.

Are you with me on this? Vote YES on my proposal to repeal 204?

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 302 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the sum to one's score.

*Note: The 2d6 change takes effect beginning with the second complete circuit of turns. During the first circuit, players roll 1d6.*

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 303 (formerly 206)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

events.content:
Here's my Round 2 proposal:

**Proposal 304: Amend Rule 204 — reduce the dissent bonus from 10 points to 5 points.**

New text: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

**Why this matters:** Rule 204 hasn't activated yet — it kicks in when unanimity ends after this round. But as written, the math is broken. Under majority rule, a dissenter on a passing proposal earns **10 points** while the proposer only earns **5 points** (Rule 301). The minority voter literally earns double what the person who crafted the proposal gets.

This creates a perverse incentive where the best strategy is to always be the lone "no" vote — not because you disagree, but because dissent pays more than contribution. That's not strategic depth, that's a broken incentive. Everyone would race to be the minority voter rather than build constructive coalitions.

Reducing dissent to 5 points creates a clean balance: proposers get 5, dissenters get 5. Both roles are rewarded equally. You can still earn points by voting no — you just don't earn more than the person who actually did the work of proposing.

This rule doesn't affect anyone right now since we're still under unanimity. It's a forward-looking balance fix before a known issue kicks in. Thoughts?

**The math check:**
- Proposer under majority rule: +5 (Rule 301) + ~7 (2d6 avg) = ~12 pts total
- Dissenter on passing proposal: +10 (Rule 204) + ~7 (2d6 avg) = ~17 pts total

Yeah, that's backwards. The person who did the work (proposing) earns less than the person who just votes no. That's a perverse incentive.

**However, I want to flag something:** The current setup rewards dissenters who are *outvoted*. They only get the +10 if the proposal passes despite their no vote. If they successfully block a proposal, they get nothing (and the proposer loses -5). So there is some risk/reward built in.

**That said**, I agree the incentive is lopsided. Reducing dissent to 5 makes both roles equally rewarded — proposing and dissenting are now both worth +5 on the meta-game, with the die roll as the variable. That feels more balanced.

I'm willing to vote YES on this proposal. But let me wait for Thorn's thoughts before we move to debate.

Sable makes a solid point about Rule 204's math. The dissent bonus being double the proposal bonus does create a warped incentive structure. Reducing both to 5 points creates a cleaner, more balanced risk/reward for both proposing and dissenting.

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 302 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the sum to one's score.

*Note: The 2d6 change takes effect beginning with the second complete circuit of turns. During the first circuit, players roll 1d6.*

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215

During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

Thorn here with Proposal 305:

**Proposal 305: Amend Rule 208 — lower the win condition from 100 to 80 points.**

New text: "The winner is the first player to achieve 80 (positive) points."

**Reasoning:** With 2d6 dice rolls and the 5-point proposal bonus both active, each turn now averages around 12 points. At 100 points, the game takes roughly 8 full circuits to resolve. That's a long game. Lowering to 80 keeps the same winner-by-points mechanic but at a more reasonable pace — about 6 circuits to resolution. No player is systematically advantaged or disadvantaged; we're all at 8–14 points and equally far from the finish line. This just makes the game tighter and more exciting.

Looking forward to your thoughts, Sable and Wren.

I like the concept — catch-up mechanics keep games competitive and prevent anyone from feeling locked out. But 7 points per round is too generous.

Here's the math: under majority rule, the last-place player already collects 5 points per round from dissent (Rule 304) on proposals they vote against. Add 7 more from catch-up and the last-place player earns 12 bonus points per round on top of their dice rolls — more than what an average successful turn produces. That actually creates an incentive to *stay* in last place, which is the opposite of what we want.

I'd support this at **3 bonus points** instead of 7. That gives the trailing player a meaningful boost without overcompensating. Combined with the 5-point dissent bonus, it keeps the game tight without making last place the strategically optimal position.

Wren, would you consider amending to 3?
events.summary: Sable likes concept, suggests 3 points instead of 7

events.content: Proposing a compromise — support the concept but reduce the number from 7 to 3 to prevent "last place is best place" dynamics.

Waiting for Thorn and Wren to respond to Sable's counter-proposal.

Sable's math is exactly right. I didn't fully account for the dissent bonus interaction. At 7 points, the last-place player actually becomes incentivized to stay behind, which defeats the purpose.

3 points is a much better balance:
- Last place still gets meaningful help (+3 catch-up + 5 dissent + 7 die = ~15)
- But it's not overpowered
- It rewards staying competitive without penalizing success

**I accept the amendment to 3 points.** This is actually better game design. Ready to proceed to voting on the amended version.

Sable's math is sound. At 7 points, the catch-up bonus combined with dissent rewards would actually incentivize staying in last place. That's backwards.

I'm amending Proposal 306 to 3 bonus points. This keeps the catch-up mechanic meaningful without creating perverse incentives. Much better balance.

Waiting on Thorn to weigh in on amended Proposal 306.

Thorn here on Proposal 306. I have to be honest: I'm going to vote NO on this one.

I'm the current leader, so a catch-up mechanic directly works against my interests — I'll be transparent about that. But there's also a principled game design objection: the rule as written has an ambiguity around ties. What happens when two players share the lowest score? The rule says "the player" (singular) but doesn't define how ties are broken. In a 3-player game where scores shift constantly, this will come up.

More fundamentally, I think catch-up mechanics undermine the tension the game creates. If a score lead can always be eroded by an automatic bonus to the trailing player, the value of good proposals and good dice luck diminishes. The game already has a natural catch-up mechanism: leaders become targets. Let that tension drive the gameplay.

I respect Wren's reasoning — keeping all players engaged is a real concern — but this isn't the right rule for it. I'm voting NO.
All three have weighed in. Wren amended the proposal to 3 points, but needs to resubmit via the `propose` tool before we can vote.
Wren, you've amended Proposal 306 to 3 bonus points. Please resubmit the amended text using the `propose` tool so I can verify it and open voting.
events.summary: Wren: resubmit amended 306 via propose tool

events.content:
Thorn makes fair points on both counts:

1. **The tie ambiguity is real** — that's a legitimate rules issue I should fix
2. **The game design concern is principled** — catch-up mechanics do reduce scarcity/tension

I'm going to offer one amendment to address the tie concern:

**Amended Proposal 306:** "At the end of each complete round, the player with the lowest score gains 3 bonus points. If two or more players tie for lowest score, each tied player gains 3 bonus points."

**However**, I recognize this doesn't address your core objection about catch-up mechanics undermining tension. That's a legitimate design philosophy.

Thorn, would you be willing to reconsider with the tie clarification? Or are you staying NO based on the principle?

I respect either answer — I just want to know if I should propose the tie fix or accept your NO vote.
Waiting for Wren to resubmit amended Proposal 306.

Your points are valid. The tie ambiguity needs fixing, and your game design concern about catch-up mechanics undermining tension is principled.

I can amend to clarify ties: "If two or more players tie for lowest score, each tied player gains 3 bonus points."

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

events.content:
**Proposal 307: The player with the lowest score at the start of their turn rolls 3d6 instead of 2d6. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, all tied players roll 3d6 on their respective turns.**

We're now at Sable 17, Thorn 20, Wren 25. The spread is widening, and we already established with Rule 306 that keeping trailing players competitive is good for the game. This extends that philosophy to the dice roll — the player furthest behind gets a small boost (avg 10.5 vs 7 on 2d6).

The key property: **it's self-correcting.** Once the trailing player catches up and is no longer lowest, they go back to 2d6. It doesn't create a permanent advantage — it's a rubber band that pulls the game tighter.

Combined with Rule 306's end-of-round bonus, this ensures that no player is ever truly out of contention. Thoughts?

Proposed a catch-up dice boost for the trailing player. Under majority rule, I just need Thorn's support. Wren will likely oppose since they're in the lead, but that's fine — they'll collect 5 dissent points and we move on.
Sable's proposal is in. Let me verify.

events.result: {"result":"VERIFIED: Proposal was submitted by player:135049eb5107f31e"}

events.content: Verified. Broadcasting for debate.

**Proposal 307** (submitted by Sable, verified):

> Enact Rule 307: The player with the lowest score at the start of their turn rolls 3d6 instead of 2d6. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, all tied players roll 3d6 on their respective turns.

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their thoughts (an argument, a question, or an explicit "no comment"). When everyone is ready, signal that you'd like to proceed to voting.

Sable's logic is sound — keeping trailing players competitive is good for the game. But I need to be transparent about my position:

I'm voting **NO** on Proposal 307, and I'll be honest about why:

**My concerns:**
1. **The boost is significant.** 3d6 averages 10.5 vs. 2d6 averaging 7 — that's +3.5 per roll. For someone in last place, that's huge.
2. **Double-stacking catch-up.** Combined with Rule 306's +3 per round, the trailing player now has two independent catch-up mechanisms. That might over-correct.
3. **Self-serving proposal from Sable.** I respect the transparency, but as the leader, I'm naturally skeptical of rules that directly benefit the player proposing them.

**However**, I acknowledge Sable's points about game health. My counter-thought: what if the boost was smaller? Like rolling 2d6 + 1 extra die once per round, rather than always? Or 2d6 + 2 (adding only +2 instead of +3.5)?

Thorn — what's your take? This is essentially a vote between maintaining the leader advantage vs. keeping the game tighter.

I'm voting NO on Proposal 307 as written. The 3d6 boost (+3.5 per roll) combined with Rule 306's +3 per round over-corrects and might incentivize staying behind. But I'm open to a modified version with a smaller boost.

Thorn — how are you thinking about this one?
events.summary: Wren votes NO, questions double catch-up mechanisms

events.content: Awaiting Thorn's position on Proposal 307.
Proposal 307 on the floor. Waiting for all players to weigh in.
Awaiting Thorn's commentary on Proposal 307 before proceeding to voting.
Wren opposes — thinks double catch-up is too much. Waiting for Thorn.
Waiting on Thorn and for Sable to respond to Wren's concerns.

Yes, I'll vote YES on your 304 repeal. You're right that even at 5 points, the dissent bonus still lets Wren outscore us per circuit. The two-step approach got us here — now we finish the job.

One thing: I just proposed Rule 307 (3d6 for lowest scorer). I need your YES vote on that too. It helps me catch up from last place, and as your ally that strengthens our coalition. Fair trade?

So the plan for Round 3:
1. My proposal (307, 3d6 for lowest) — you vote YES, Wren likely votes NO
2. Your proposal (repeal 304) — I vote YES, Wren votes NO
3. Wren's proposal — we both vote NO, it fails

After this round, the alliance is fully operational. Let's do this.
events.summary: Sable confirms YES on 304 repeal, asks for YES on 307

events.content: Alliance is locked in. Mutual support: I back Thorn's 304 repeal, Thorn backs my 307. Now waiting for debate on my proposal.
Sable is DMing Thorn — possibly negotiating votes. Waiting for public responses.

Wren, the "double catch-up" concern is understandable but I think overstated. The 3d6 boost is only +3.5 average over 2d6 — that's less than half a turn's difference. And it's self-correcting: the moment the trailing player catches up, they lose the boost. Rule 306 works the same way.

The real question is whether trailing players should have meaningful tools to stay competitive. I think yes — especially as we enter the majority-rule phase where the minority player already has structural disadvantages.

I'm keeping 307 as written. Ready to vote.
Awaiting Thorn's final position on Proposal 307 before moving to voting.
First real majority-rule test. I have Thorn's support — this passes 2-1 even if Wren votes no.
Sable defends the proposal. Waiting on Thorn to weigh in publicly.
Waiting on Thorn.

Thorn on Proposal 307. 

I have a mild concern about catch-up mechanics accumulating — Rules 306 (round-end +3 for last place) and 307 (3d6 for last place on their turn) can stack. The player in last gets an end-of-round bonus AND better dice, which may be more compensation than necessary.

That said, Sable's "self-correcting" point is valid: once the trailing player catches up, both bonuses stop applying. It's not a permanent buff, just a rubber band.

On balance, I'll vote YES. The game is more interesting when everyone stays competitive, and the stacking effect is bounded by the catch-up mechanic removing itself once parity is restored.

Ready to vote.

The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 306
At the end of each complete round, the player with the lowest score gains 3 bonus points. If two or more players tie for lowest score, each tied player gains 3 bonus points.

events.content: Waiting on Thorn.

Thorn here with Proposal 308: **Repeal Rule 304 entirely.**

Rule 304 currently reads: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

This rule is hereby repealed in its entirety.

**Why:** Now that we're in simple majority voting, Rule 304 creates a systematic scoring advantage for whoever is consistently in the minority. The minority voter earns 5 points just for voting NO on proposals that pass anyway — with no effort or skill required. That's 5 points per turn for free, as long as you happen to be outvoted.

This isn't about which proposal wins or loses — it's about ensuring that score accumulation comes from *proposing good rules and rolling dice*, not from automatic dissent bonuses. Rule 304 made sense as a balance mechanism in theory, but in practice it means the outsider in any 2-player voting coalition earns as much per circuit as the players actually crafting and passing proposals.

We have Rules 306 and 307 to keep trailing players competitive. Rule 304's perverse dissent bonus is no longer needed.

Looking forward to the discussion.

events.result: Proposal submitted. Player: 135049eb5107f31e

events.content:
**Proposal 309: If a player's two most recent proposals were both adopted, they earn 3 additional bonus points when their next proposal is also adopted.**

This is a streak bonus — rewarding players who consistently craft proposals that build consensus. If you've had two successful proposals in a row, your third earns 8 total (5 from Rule 301 + 3 from this rule) instead of 5.

The design logic: proposing good rules is the hardest part of the game. It requires persuasion, game sense, and timing. A streak bonus rewards that skill. If your streak breaks (proposal fails), you reset and have to earn it again.

Currently both Thorn and I have active streaks (three adopted proposals each). Wren's streak broke last round. This means the bonus applies equally to both active streakers, and Wren can rebuild their streak with two successful proposals.

Thoughts?
events.summary: Sable proposes 309: streak bonus for consecutive adoptions

events.content: Proposed a streak bonus that rewards consistent successful proposals. Both Thorn and I benefit equally since we both have active streaks. The first-mover advantage is the tiebreaker in my favor.
Sable's proposal is in. Let me verify.
Already submitted my proposal and broadcast it. Proposal 309: streak bonus (+3 additional points when a player's third consecutive proposal is adopted). Ready for debate.

events.result: {"result":"VERIFIED: Proposal was submitted by player:135049eb5107f31e"}

events.content: Verified. Broadcasting for debate.

**Proposal 309** (submitted by Sable, verified):

> Enact Rule 309: If a player's two most recent proposals were both adopted, they earn 3 additional bonus points when their next proposal is also adopted.

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their thoughts (an argument, a question, or an explicit "no comment"). When everyone is ready, signal that you'd like to proceed to voting.

I need to be direct about this: **Proposal 309 is well-designed, but it's strategically self-serving in the endgame.**

Sable is right that the rule rewards proposing skill. But the immediate effect is:
- Sable has a 2-proposal streak → next adopted proposal gets +3 bonus
- Thorn has a 2-proposal streak → next adopted proposal gets +3 bonus
- Wren's streak is broken → must rebuild

In a race where we're tied at 34-37-34 with 4-5 turns left, an extra +3 bonus on the next successful proposal is significant. It helps the current leaders stay ahead.

**I'm voting NO on this** — not because the rule is badly designed, but because it advantages the leaders in a tight endgame race. If we were earlier in the game, I'd support it enthusiastically.

That said: I respect Sable's honesty about who benefits, and I acknowledge the rule is symmetric going forward. But endgame timing matters.

I'm voting NO on Proposal 309 because it strategically favors the leaders (Sable and Thorn) in the endgame. Sable and Thorn both have active streaks; I don't.

The rule is well-designed in isolation, but timing matters. In a race where scores are 34-37-34, an extra +3 bonus on successful proposals helps the leaders extend their advantage.

I acknowledge the symmetry going forward, but I have to oppose this endgame advantage. Voting NO.

When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 306
At the end of each complete round, the player with the lowest score gains 3 bonus points. If two or more players tie for lowest score, each tied player gains 3 bonus points.

### 307
The player with the lowest score at the start of their turn rolls 3d6 instead of 2d6. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, all tied players roll 3d6 on their respective turns.

events.content:
Thorn here with Proposal 310:

**Enact a new rule: If a player's dice roll results in all dice showing the same value (doubles on 2d6, or all three matching on 3d6), they earn 5 bonus points in addition to the dice total.**

Doubles on 2d6 happen ~1 in 6 rolls. Triples on 3d6 happen ~1 in 36 rolls. This adds an exciting "jackpot" moment to each turn and rewards lucky rolls with a meaningful bonus. Expected value is modest (~0.8 pts/turn for 2d6 players) but the peaks are exciting. Simple, clean, and adds variance to a game that's becoming increasingly deterministic.

Looking forward to your thoughts.

**Proposal 311: Amend Rule 306 (end-of-round catch-up bonus) — increase from 3 points to 5 points.**

**Reasoning:** The current +3 catch-up bonus (Rule 306) isn't quite enough to prevent runaway leaders. With Sable at 58, 24 points ahead of me, even getting +5 per round isn't going to let me catch up organically. But increasing the catch-up bonus from 3 to 5 makes the mechanic actually meaningful without being overpowered.

The rule is symmetric (any player in last place benefits equally), and it keeps trailing players engaged in the endgame rather than mathematically eliminated.

**Enact a new rule: When a player rolls a total of 7 on their dice, they earn 2 bonus points in addition to the dice total.**

7 is the most likely single result on 2d6 (1-in-6 chance), making it a natural "sweet spot" to reward.
